step_by_step_thinking: I agree with the chosen option of 28-30°C (Option B), primarily because this temperature range effectively balances the need to prevent hypothermia, which could cause serious health issues like bradycardia and apnea, against the potential dangers of overheating, such as increased metabolic demands and dehydration. Additionally, studies have shown that infants maintained within this narrower range exhibit better outcomes regarding body temperature regulation and energy conservation post-birth. Thus, my final answer remains Option B. .
